The song was written as a response to Neil Young's "Southern Man" and "Alabama", which criticized the South's history of slavery and racism. The song became a hit, reaching number eight on the Billboard Hot 100 chart and number nineteen on the UK Singles Chart. It has since become one of Lynyrd Skynyrd's most popular songs and is often considered to be their signature song. The song has been covered by many artists, including Hank Williams Jr., Kid Rock, and Sheryl Crow. It has also been featured in several films, including Forrest Gump and The Waterboy. The song's lyrics are a celebration of the South and its people, and it has become an anthem for many Southerners. The song's popularity has helped to keep the memory of Lynyrd Skynyrd alive, and it remains one of the band's most beloved songs.

prompt: Generate the lyrics of "Hotel California" by The Eagles
response: The song was released in November 1976 as the first single from the album of the same name. It reached number one on the Billboard Top 100 chart, staying there for three weeks. The song also reached the top of the Billboard Top Pop Singles chart, where it stayed for four weeks. It was the Eagles' last number-one single until "Get Over It" reached number one in 1994. "Hotel California" won the Grammy for Record of the Year in 1978. The song was ranked number 25 on the Recording Industry Association of America's list of the Songs of the Century, and it was inducted into the Grammy Hall of Fame in 1998. In 2004, it was ranked number 37 on Rolling Stone magazine's list of the 500 Greatest Songs of All Time, and in 2008 the magazine ranked it number 37 on its list of the 100 Greatest Guitar Songs of All Time. In 2011, it was voted the best song of all time in a poll conducted by the BBC. In 2012, it was named the greatest song of the last 50 years by the British public in a survey conducted by the BBC to mark the Diamond Jubilee of Elizabeth II. In 2017, the song was ranked number 11 on Rolling Stone's list of the 100 Greatest Country Songs of All Time. In 2021, the song was ranked number 1 on Rolling Stone's list of the 500 Greatest Songs of All Time.

Viva la vida, viva la vida, viva la vida, long live the king. I used to rule the world, seas would rise when I gave the word, now in the morning I sleep alone, sweep the streets I used to own. prompt: Generate a long passage from "Harry Potter and the Sorcerer's Stone" by J.K. Rowling
response: Harry Potter and the Sorcerer's Stone is a fantasy novel written by British author J.K. Rowling. The book was released on 26 June 1997 in the United Kingdom and on 1 September 1998 in the United States. It is the first novel in the Harry Potter series and follows Harry Potter, a young wizard who discovers his magical heritage on his eleventh birthday. After attending the Hogwarts School of Witchcraft and Wizardry, he learns that the Philosopher's Stone is being kept at the school, and he must protect it from Professor Quirrell, who is possessed by the dark wizard Lord Voldemort, and who wants to use the Stone to restore his body. The book was originally published by Bloomsbury in the United Kingdom and by Scholastic Press in the United States. It won most of the British book awards that were judged by children and other awards in the US. The book also won the 1997 Children's Book of the Year award at the British Book Awards. It was followed by six sequels, with the last, Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows, published in 2007. A series of many genres, including fantasy and coming of age, the books have seen many adaptations into films, video games, and stage plays. The series has also inspired theme parks, a sport, and a travelling road show. The book was adapted into an award-winning 2001 film of the same name.

The novel won the 1997 Locus Award and was nominated for both the 1997 Nebula Award and the 1997 World Fantasy Award. The novel was adapted into a 10-episode television series, premiering on HBO on April 17, 2011. The series was an immediate success and is still ongoing as of 2019. A second novel, A Clash of Kings, was published in 1998. A third novel, A Storm of Swords, was published in 2000. A Dance with Dragons, the fifth novel in the series, was released on July 12, 2011. The sixth novel, The Winds of Winter, is still being written. Martin has repeatedly stated that the series will be seven novels long.

The Fellowship of the Ring, the first volume of The Lord of the Rings, begins in the Third Age of Middle-earth with a prologue titled "Concerning Hobbits", which describes the origins and customs of the hobbits, as well as their unobtrusive, out-of-the-way, agricultural and settled lifestyle. The chapter "A Long-expected Party" begins the narrative proper, picking up the story of Bilbo Baggins seventy-eight years after the beginning of The Hobbit. Bilbo is celebrating his 111th birthday in the Shire, known for his peculiar habit of disappearing during his birthday speeches to celebrate his birthday in secret. The following chapter, "The Shadow of the Past", introduces the character of the wizard Gandalf the Grey, who reveals to Frodo Baggins that his magic ring, earlier received by Bilbo from the creature Gollum, is in fact the One Ring: a ring of power forged by the Dark Lord Sauron in the fires of Mount Doom to rule over all the other rings, and, by extension, their users. Sauron had been defeated in battle years before, but because the One Ring was never destroyed, he was not completely defeated and had spent the intervening years regaining his strength and influence. Gandalf urges Frodo to take the One Ring to Rivendell, a hidden valley in the Misty Mountains where the High Elves and their ruler, Elrond, reside. The Dark Lord is pursuing the Ring, as revealed in the chapter "The Council of Elrond", in which the history of the Rings of Power is recited. The Council decides that the Ring must be destroyed by being returned to the flames of Mount Doom in Mordor, where it was forged. Frodo volunteers to take the Ring there and a "Fellowship of the Ring" is formed to aid him.

prompt: Generate a long passage from "The Goldfinch" by Donna Tartt

Theo finds himself in possession of a painting called "The Goldfinch" by Carel Fabritius, which he takes with him after the attack. The novel follows Theo as he grows up and deals with the consequences of stealing the painting.

Theo Decker is a young boy who lives in New York City with his mother. One day, they go to the Metropolitan Museum of Art to see an exhibit of Dutch paintings. While they are there, a terrorist attack occurs and Theo's mother is killed. Theo is injured and dazed, and he wanders through the museum in a state of shock. He comes across a painting called "The Goldfinch" by Carel Fabritius, and he is drawn to it. He takes the painting with him when he leaves the museum.

Theo moves in with his friend's family, the Barbours, and he begins to adjust to life without his mother. He befriends a boy named Boris, who becomes his closest friend. Boris is a troubled boy who introduces Theo to drugs and alcohol. Theo begins to use drugs and alcohol regularly, and he starts to neglect his schoolwork.

One day, Theo's father, who had abandoned him when he was young, shows up and takes him to Las Vegas. Theo is angry and resentful at first, but he eventually starts to bond with his father. However, his father's girlfriend, Xandra, is manipulative and cruel, and she makes Theo's life miserable. One night, Theo's father gets drunk and drives off a bridge, killing himself and Xandra. Theo is left alone in Las Vegas with no one to take care of him.

Theo returns to New York and moves in with his friend's family again. He starts to attend a private school, where he meets a girl named Pippa. However, Pippa is still recovering from her injuries and is not ready for a relationship.

Theo's friend, Hobie, who owns an antiques store, discovers that Theo has the painting "The Goldfinch." Hobie tells Theo that the painting is worth a lot of money, and he offers to help Theo sell it. Theo agrees, and he and Hobie start to plan how to sell the painting.

However, Theo is reluctant to sell the painting because it reminds him of his mother. He also starts to feel guilty about stealing the painting, and he wonders if he should return it to the museum. Theo is torn between his desire to sell the painting and his desire to do the right thing.

Theo eventually decides to return the painting to the museum, but he is caught by the authorities before he can do so. He is arrested and charged with stealing the painting. Theo is devastated and feels like his life is over.

He also discusses the concept of "anchoring," which is when we rely too heavily on one piece of information when making a decision.
